title: Enterococcal Infections
authors: William R. Miller, Cesar A. Arias, Barbara E. Murray $ ^{*} $

items.title: Identifying Enterococci: Key Differentiating Features
items.content: Enterococci are gram-positive organisms that can be distinguished from streptococci by their ability to hydrolyze esculin in the presence of 40% bile salts and grow at high salt concentrations (6.5%) and temperatures (46°C). They also typically hydrolyze pyrrolidonyl-β-naphthylamide (PYR), which aids in differentiating them from other similar organisms.
keyPoints: Enterococci are gram-positive, appearing as single cells, diplococci, or short chains.
They can hydrolyze esculin in 40% bile salts and grow at 6.5% salt concentration and 46°C.
PYR hydrolysis is a key differentiator from Streptococcus gallolyticus group and Leuconostoc species.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · Enterococcal Infections | MICROBIOLOGY AND TAXONOMY

items.title: Clinical Relevance of Enterococci Species
items.content: The majority of clinically relevant enterococcal infections are caused by E. faecalis and E. faecium, with other species like E. gallinarum, E. durans, E. hirae, and E. avium being less frequently isolated.
keyPoints: E. faecalis and E. faecium are the predominant species in clinical infections.
Other species include E. gallinarum, E. durans, E. hirae, and E. avium.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · Enterococcal Infections | MICROBIOLOGY AND TAXONOMY

items.title: Role of Enterococci in Colonization Resistance
items.content: Enterococci, as commensals in the human gut, contribute to colonization resistance by preventing the establishment of drug-resistant bacteria like VRE. This resistance is due to complex metabolic and immunologic interactions that are disrupted in hospitalized or chronically ill patients.
keyPoints: Enterococci make up <1% of the culturable intestinal microbiota.
Colonization resistance involves metabolic and immunologic interactions.
Disruption occurs in hospitalized or chronically ill patients.

items.title: Impact of Antimicrobial Agents on Enterococcal Colonization
items.content: Antimicrobial agents, especially those excreted in bile with broad-spectrum activity, promote enterococcal colonization. This is not just due to niche filling but involves host-derived antimicrobial peptides and microbiota-produced compounds like lantibiotics.
keyPoints: Antibiotics increase enterococcal recovery from feces.
Host-derived antimicrobial peptides (e.g., RegIII $ \gamma $) are crucial for colonization resistance.
Lantibiotics from commensals can be active against VRE.

items.title: Enterococcal Influence on Noninfectious Syndromes
items.content: Enterococci, particularly E. faecium, E. durans, and E. hirae, can modulate the host immune response, affecting conditions like graft-versus-host disease and enhancing anti-PD-L1 therapy through the action of salA.
keyPoints: Enterococcal domination in gut linked to increased inflammation in graft-versus-host disease.
SalA gene mediates enhanced antitumor immune response via NF- $ \kappa B $ activation.

items.title: Enterococcal Secreted Factors and Their Role in Virulence
items.content: Enterococci secrete factors like enterococcal hemolysin/cytolysin, gelatinase, serine protease, and Epx that contribute to their pathogenicity. These molecules facilitate infection by lysing host cells, degrading tissues, modifying immune components, and targeting specific human antigens.
keyPoints: Enterococcal cytolysin is a heterodimeric toxin capable of lysing human RBCs and polymorphonuclear leukocytes.
Epx targets human leukocyte antigen type 1 and acts against peripheral blood mononuclear cells and intestinal organoids in vitro.

Gelatinase and serine protease degrade host tissues and modify immune system components, with mutants lacking these genes being highly attenuated in models of peritonitis, endocarditis, and endophthalmitis.

items.title: Enterococcal Surface Components and Their Contribution to Virulence
items.content: Surface components such as adhesins facilitate bacterial attachment to host tissues, enhancing virulence. Key molecules include aggregation substance, Ace, Acm, pili, Esp, Scm, Fms, SgrA, EcbA, Erl protein, polysaccharides, and teichoic acids.
keyPoints: Aggregation substance mediates bacterial cell attachment, facilitating plasmid exchange and increasing virulence in endocarditis.
Ace and Acm are MSCRAMMs that recognize collagen, fibronectin, and fibrinogen, crucial for pathogenesis in experimental endocarditis.
Pili mutants show attenuation in biofilm production, endocarditis, and UTIs.
Teichoic acids are immunogenic; antibodies to them provide protection in some animal models.

items.title: Less Characterized Virulence Factors of Enterococci
items.content: Factors like the E. faecalis stress protein Gls24, hylEfm-containing plasmids, and AsrR contribute to resistance and virulence in specific conditions such as bile salt resistance and oxidative stress regulation.
keyPoints: Gls24 is associated with enterococcal resistance to bile salts and plays a role in endocarditis pathogenesis.
hylEfm-containing plasmids increase gastrointestinal colonization by E. faecium and enhance lethality in mouse peritonitis models.
AsrR regulates oxidative stress, acting as an important virulence factor for E. faecium.

items.title: Enterococcal Species and Resistance Trends
items.content: Enterococci are the third most common hospital-associated infections in the U.S., with E. faecium showing a significant rise in isolation, now accounting for about one-third of enterococcal infections. E. faecium is notably resistant to ampicillin (over 90% resistance) and vancomycin (50–70% in acute care hospitals).
keyPoints: Third most common hospital-associated infection after Escherichia coli and staphylococci.
E. faecium accounts for ~one-third of enterococcal infections.
>90% resistance to ampicillin in E. faecium.
50–70% vancomycin resistance in E. faecium in acute care hospitals.

items.title: VRE Colonization and Transmission
items.content: VRE colonization is a critical precursor to enterococcal disease, with prolonged colonization increasing the risk of illness. Factors like long hospital stays, antibiotic use, and proximity to VRE patients enhance colonization risks. VRE's resilience on surfaces aids its transmission.
keyPoints: VRE colonization can last >1 year.
Increased likelihood of Enterococcus-related illnesses in colonized patients.
Factors: prolonged hospitalization, long-term antibiotic use, high APACHE scores.
VRE survives on various hospital surfaces.

items.title: Global Trends in VRE Emergence
items.content: The emergence of VRE varies globally. In Europe, initial increases were linked to avoparcin use in animals; bans led to temporary declines but subsequent rises in hospital-associated infections. Rates differ regionally, with lower resistance in western Europe compared to southern and eastern regions.
keyPoints: Initial VRE emergence in Europe linked to avoparcin in animal feeds.
European Union banned avoparcin in 1996.
Hospital-associated VRE increased from 8.1% (2012) to 17.6% (2022).
Regional differences: <5–10% resistance in western Europe, 25–50% in southern/eastern.

items.title: Genomic Plasticity in Enterococcal Evolution
items.content: The ability to sequence bacterial genomes has enhanced our understanding of enterococci's evolutionary trajectory, highlighting their genomic plasticity. This includes the recombination of large portions of chromosomal DNA and a malleable accessory genome with plasmids, phages, and mobile genetic elements.
keyPoints: Genus appeared ~400 million years ago with terrestrial animals.
Genomic plasticity contributes to rising antibiotic resistance rates.
Accessory genome includes plasmids, phages, and mobile genetic elements.

items.title: Differences Between Enterococcus Clades
items.content: Enterococcal isolates are divided into two clades: hospital-associated clade A and community-associated clade B. Clade A evolves rapidly through recombination events, often lacking a functional CRISPR-Cas system, which contributes to its higher frequency of resistance determinants.
keyPoints: Clade A is associated with invasive disease and drug resistance.
Clade B retains a susceptible phenotype.
Clade A lacks a functional CRISPR-Cas system, aiding in resistance acquisition.
Clade A isolates possess alleles of PBP5 linked to high-level β-lactam resistance.

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Enterococcal UTIs
items.content: Enterococci are common causes of nosocomial urinary tract infections, often linked to catheterization or genitourinary abnormalities. Differentiating between infection and colonization is challenging, especially in patients with chronic indwelling catheters. A positive urine culture with $ \geq10^{5} $ CFU/mL suggests true infection when accompanied by systemic symptoms like fever.
keyPoints: Enterococci are common causes of nosocomial UTIs.
Differentiating between infection and colonization is challenging in patients with chronic catheters.
A positive urine culture with $ \geq10^{5} $ CFU/mL suggests true infection when systemic symptoms are present.

items.title: Enterococcal Bacteremia and Endocarditis
items.content: Enterococci frequently cause bacteremia, often associated with intravascular catheters. E. faecium is linked to worse outcomes due to higher resistance rates to vancomycin and ampicillin. Enterococci are significant causes of endocarditis, especially after procedures like TAVI, affecting mainly elderly males with comorbidities.
keyPoints: Enterococci are common causes of bacteremia, often linked to catheters.
E. faecium is associated with worse outcomes due to higher resistance rates.
Enterococci cause significant endocarditis cases, especially after TAVI.

items.title: Management of Enterococcal Endocarditis
items.content: Enterococcal endocarditis typically presents with subacute symptoms like fever and cardiac murmur. Treatment usually lasts 4–6 weeks, but longer courses are needed for multidrug-resistant strains without valve replacement. Surgery reduces relapse risk compared to antibiotic treatment alone.
keyPoints: Enterococcal endocarditis often presents with subacute symptoms.
Treatment duration is typically 4–6 weeks; longer for resistant strains without surgery.
Surgery reduces relapse risk compared to antibiotics alone.

items.title: Enterococcal Meningitis: Recognizing the Uncommon but Serious
items.content: Enterococcal meningitis is a rare condition, accounting for about 4% of all meningitis cases. It often occurs in patients with predisposing factors like neurosurgical interventions or debilitating conditions such as cardiovascular disease and immunosuppression. Key CSF findings include pleocytosis (average ~500/μL), elevated protein (>100 mg/dL), and decreased glucose (average 28 mg/dL). Complications can be severe, including hydrocephalus and brain abscesses.
keyPoints: Enterococcal meningitis accounts for ~4% of meningitis cases.
Common in patients with neurosurgical interventions or debilitating conditions.
CSF findings: pleocytosis (~500/μL), protein >100 mg/dL, glucose 28 mg/dL.
Complications include hydrocephalus, brain abscesses, and stroke.

items.title: Intraabdominal Infections: The Rising Role of Enterococci
items.content: Enterococci are increasingly significant in intraabdominal infections, especially in hospitalized patients due to vancomycin resistance and multidrug-resistant E. faecium. Anti-enterococcal therapy is recommended for nosocomial peritonitis in high-risk patients. However, specific treatment for community-acquired infections in healthy individuals without cardiac risk factors may not be beneficial.
keyPoints: Enterococci are a growing concern in intraabdominal infections due to vancomycin resistance.
Anti-enterococcal therapy is advised for nosocomial peritonitis in high-risk patients.
Treatment for community-acquired infections in healthy individuals without cardiac risk factors may not be necessary.

items.title: Enterococcal Infections in Soft Tissue and Bone: Challenges and Considerations
items.content: Enterococci are common in soft tissue infections, ranking third in nosocomial surgical-site infections. They can complicate bone infections, especially with underlying conditions like diabetes or endocarditis. The presence of biofilms on foreign material complicates treatment, often necessitating hardware removal to eradicate the infection.
keyPoints: Enterococci are common in soft tissue and nosocomial surgical-site infections.
Biofilm production by enterococci can complicate bone infections involving hardware.
Hardware removal may be necessary for effective treatment of such infections.

items.title: Why Combination Therapy is Standard for Enterococcal Endocarditis
items.content: Enterococci exhibit intrinsic resistance and/or tolerance to many antimicrobial agents, making monotherapy with $\beta$-lactam antibiotics ineffective due to high relapse rates. The addition of an aminoglycoside to a cell wall-active agent (either a $\beta$-lactam or glycopeptide) enhances cure rates and eradicates the organisms by synergistically increasing bacterial cell penetration.
keyPoints: Enterococci are intrinsically resistant/tolerant to several antimicrobial agents.
Tolerance is defined as lack of killing by drug concentrations 32 times higher than the minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC).
Monotherapy with $\beta$-lactam antibiotics results in high relapse rates.
Combination therapy with a cell wall-active agent and an aminoglycoside increases cure rates and eradicates organisms.
Synergistic bactericidal activity is achieved by increased penetration of the aminoglycoside into the bacterial cell.

items.title: Treatment Considerations for Enterococcal Infections Based on Species and Resistance Profiles
items.content: The treatment approach for enterococcal infections differs significantly between E. faecalis and E. faecium due to their resistance profiles. While ampicillin and vancomycin are rarely resisted by E. faecalis, they are often ineffective against E. faecium. In cases of endocarditis caused by multidrug-resistant enterococci, valve replacement may be necessary. For less severe infections related to indwelling intravascular catheters, removing the catheter enhances the effectiveness of antimicrobial therapy.
keyPoints: Treatment differs between E. faecalis and E. faecium due to resistance profiles.
Resistance to ampicillin and vancomycin is rare in E. faecalis but common in E. faecium.
Valve replacement may be necessary for endocarditis caused by multidrug-resistant enterococci.
Catheter removal increases the likelihood of enterococcal eradication with appropriate antimicrobial therapy.

items.title: Optimal Antibiotic Regimens for Enterococcal Infections
items.content: For E. faecalis infections, the combination of ampicillin and ceftriaxone is recommended over ampicillin plus gentamicin due to comparable efficacy with reduced toxicity risks.
keyPoints: Ampicillin and ceftriaxone are as effective as ampicillin plus gentamicin for E. faecalis endocarditis.
The combination of ampicillin and ceftriaxone is recommended due to lower risk of toxicity.
This regimen is particularly suitable for patients at risk for aminoglycoside toxicity or those with high-level resistance.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.title: Aminoglycosides in Enterococcal Infections
items.content: Gentamicin and streptomycin are the only recommended aminoglycosides for synergistic therapy with $\beta$-lactams due to resistance patterns.
keyPoints: Gentamicin (1–1.5 mg/kg IV q8h) and streptomycin (15 mg/kg per day IV/IM in two divided doses) are recommended aminoglycosides.
Resistance to gentamicin implies lack of synergism with tobramycin and amikacin.
Aminoglycoside monotherapy is not advised due to resistance issues.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.title: Linezolid Use in VRE Infections
items.content: Linezolid, approved for VRE infections, is recommended only as an alternative due to potential toxicities and mixed efficacy results.
keyPoints: Linezolid is FDA-approved for VRE infections but not bactericidal.
Potential toxicities include thrombocytopenia, peripheral neuropathy, optic neuritis, and lactic acidosis when used >2 weeks.
Linezolid may be considered for enterococcal meningitis and other CNS infections despite limited data.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.title: Dosing and Monitoring for Daptomycin
items.content: For treating enterococcal infections, daptomycin should be dosed at 10–12 mg/kg once daily, adjusted based on renal function. It is crucial to monitor creatine phosphokinase levels during therapy due to the risk of rhabdomyolysis.
keyPoints: Dosing: 10–12 mg/kg once daily
Adjust for renal function
Monitor creatine phosphokinase levels due to possible rhabdomyolysis
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.title: Daptomycin Efficacy and Resistance Considerations
items.content: The efficacy of daptomycin in treating VRE bacteremia is dose-dependent, with higher doses (≥10 mg/kg) showing improved outcomes compared to standard doses (6 mg/kg). Mutations associated with resistance can emerge at lower dosing regimens.
keyPoints: Dose-dependent efficacy: ≥10 mg/kg for better outcomes
Standard dose: 6 mg/kg
Resistance mutations linked to lower dosing regimens
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.title: Daptomycin Breakpoints and MIC Categories
items.content: The CLSI updated daptomycin breakpoints in 2019. For E. faecium, isolates with an MIC ≤4 mg/L are 'susceptible dose-dependent' at 8–12 mg/kg; those with an MIC ≥8 mg/L are resistant. Other enterococci have different susceptibility categories based on their MIC values.
keyPoints: E. faecium: Susceptible dose-dependent if MIC ≤4 mg/L, Resistant if MIC ≥8 mg/L
Dosing for susceptible dose-dependent: 8–12 mg/kg
Other enterococci: Susceptible if MIC ≤2 mg/L, Intermediate at 4 mg/L, Resistant if MIC ≥8 mg/L
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.title: Mechanism of Ampicillin Resistance in Enterococcus faecium
items.content: Ampicillin resistance in E. faecium is primarily due to alterations in penicillin-binding protein 5 (PBP5). This protein has a low affinity for ampicillin, enabling cell wall synthesis even when other PBPs are inhibited. In resistant strains, PBP5 undergoes amino acid changes that further decrease its affinity for ampicillin and increase the hydrolysis of the antibiotic at the active site, facilitating higher enzyme turnover. Additionally, high-level resistance (MIC >32 µg/mL) is associated with increased production of PBP5.
keyPoints: Ampicillin resistance in E. faecium is linked to PBP5 alterations.
PBP5 has low affinity for ampicillin and can synthesize the cell wall despite antibiotic presence.
High-level resistance (MIC >32 µg/mL) involves increased production of PBP5.

items.title: Vancomycin Resistance in Enterococcus Species
items.content: Vancomycin, a glycopeptide antibiotic, inhibits cell-wall peptidoglycan synthesis by binding to UDP-MurNAc-pentapeptides. Vancomycin resistance (VRE) was first documented in 1986 and has significantly increased globally, especially in E. faecium. Resistance involves alterations that prevent vancomycin from effectively binding its target.
keyPoints: Vancomycin inhibits cell-wall synthesis by targeting peptidoglycan precursors.
First VRE isolates documented in 1986; resistance has increased, particularly in E. faecium.

items.question: Which characteristic is used to differentiate enterococci from the Streptococcus gallolyticus group?
options: Hydrolysis of esculin in the presence of bile salts
items.explanation: Enterococci hydrolyze PYR, which differentiates them from the Streptococcus gallolyticus group.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · Enterococcal Infections | MICROBIOLOGY AND TAXONOMY

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic that distinguishes enterococci from most streptococci?
options: Nonhemolytic on ovine or bovine RBCs
items.explanation: Enterococci can grow at high salt concentrations, unlike most streptococci.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · Enterococcal Infections | MICROBIOLOGY AND TAXONOMY

items.question: What was the original name given to Enterococcus faecalis when it was first described as a pathogen?
items.explanation: The original name given was Micrococcus zymogenes.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · Enterococcal Infections | MICROBIOLOGY AND TAXONOMY

items.question: What role do enterococci play in the gastrointestinal microbiota of healthy individuals?
options: They are pathogenic bacteria causing infections.
They act as symbionts maintaining homeostatic equilibrium.
They primarily cause diarrhea and other gastrointestinal disorders.
items.explanation: Enterococci coexist with other gastrointestinal bacteria and may serve as probiotics, suggesting a role in maintaining bowel homeostasis.

items.question: Which factor is critical for the pathogenesis of enterococcal infections?
options: Presence of lactose in the diet.
items.explanation: A higher level of gastrointestinal colonization is a critical factor in the pathogenesis of enterococcal infections.

items.question: What effect does the administration of certain antibiotics have on enterococci?
options: Promotes increased gastrointestinal colonization by enterococci.
Has no impact on enterococcal populations.
Eliminates all enterococci from the gut.
items.explanation: Antimicrobial agents, especially those excreted in bile with broad-spectrum activity, promote increased colonization by enterococci.

items.question: What is the role of antimicrobial peptides like RegIII $ \gamma $ in the context of VRE colonization?
options: They have no effect on VRE.
They are important mediators of colonization resistance against VRE.
items.explanation: Antimicrobial peptides such as RegIII $ \gamma $, produced by the host's innate immune system, mediate colonization resistance against VRE.

items.question: Which enterococcal secreted factor is known to specifically target human leukocyte antigen type 1?
items.explanation: Epx is a newly described pore-forming toxin that specifically targets human leukocyte antigen type 1.

items.question: Which surface protein of E. faecalis mediates attachment to collagen and plays a role in experimental endocarditis?
items.explanation: The adhesin of collagen of E. faecalis (Ace) is a surface protein that recognizes adhesive matrix molecules like collagen, contributing to endocarditis.

items.question: What role do pili play in the pathogenesis of enterococcal infections?
options: They are involved in biofilm production and attachment to host tissues.
They modify critical components of the immune system.
items.explanation: Pili mediate attachment to and invasion of host tissues, playing a role in biofilm production and infections like endocarditis and UTIs.

items.question: Which enterococcal surface component is thought to interfere with phagocytosis by host immune cells?
items.explanation: Polysaccharides on the enterococcal surface are thought to interfere with phagocytosis of the organism by host immune cells.

items.question: Which species of Enterococcus is the most resistant and challenging to treat?
items.explanation: E. faecium is the most resistant and challenging enterococcal species to treat.

items.question: What percentage of E. faecium isolates are resistant to ampicillin?
items.explanation: More than 90% of E. faecium isolates are resistant to ampicillin.

items.question: In which type of facility is vancomycin resistance in E. faecium isolates highest?
items.explanation: Vancomycin resistance in E. faecium is up to 80% in long-term care facilities.

items.question: What was the primary reason for the emergence of VRE in Europe during the mid-1980s?
options: High levels of human antibiotic use
Use of avoparcin as a growth promoter in animal feeds
items.explanation: The emergence of VRE in Europe was primarily associated with the use of avoparcin as a growth promoter in animal feeds.

items.question: Which clade of E. faecium is more frequently associated with invasive disease and drug resistance?
items.explanation: Strains belonging to clade A are more frequently identified as isolates causing invasive disease and carry drug resistance determinants.

items.question: What is a major factor contributing to the rapid evolution of hospital-associated E. faecium strains?
items.explanation: The hospital-associated clade appears to be evolving rapidly via a series of historic and ongoing recombination events.

items.question: What is one reason for the propensity of clade A strains to acquire resistance determinants?
options: Lack of a functional CRISPR-Cas system
items.explanation: Clade A strains more frequently lack a functional CRISPR-Cas system, which reduces the acquisition of resistance genes.

items.question: Which of the following is a common cause of nosocomial urinary tract infections (UTIs) caused by enterococci?
options: Healthy premenopausal women without indwelling catheters
Absence of anatomic abnormalities in the genitourinary tract
items.explanation: Enterococcal UTIs are commonly associated with indwelling catheterization, instrumentation, or anatomic abnormalities.

items.question: What is suggested by the presence of leukocytes in urine along with systemic manifestations and a positive urine culture in enterococcal UTIs?
options: Absence of any urinary tract issues
items.explanation: The presence of leukocytes in the urine with systemic manifestations and a positive culture suggests true infection.

items.question: Which statement is true regarding enterococcal bacteremia?
options: E. faecium isolation leads to better outcomes than other species.
Intravascular catheters are commonly associated with these episodes.
Enterococci are the leading cause of community-associated endocarditis.
items.explanation: Intravascular catheters and other devices are commonly associated with enterococcal bacteremia without endocarditis.

items.question: What is a typical presentation of enterococcal endocarditis?
options: Rapid onset of severe chest pain
Subacute course with fever, weight loss, and cardiac murmur
Immediate heart failure without prior symptoms
items.explanation: Enterococcal endocarditis typically presents as a subacute course with fever, weight loss, malaise, and a cardiac murmur.

items.question: Which organisms are most commonly isolated in cases of enterococcal meningitis?
items.explanation: The passage states that the most common species isolated in enterococcal meningitis are E. faecalis and E. faecium.

items.question: What is a common complication of enterococcal meningitis?
items.explanation: The passage lists hydrocephalus as one of the complications of enterococcal meningitis.

items.question: In which patients is presumed hematogenous seeding of the meninges seen in enterococcal infections?
options: Patients with cardiovascular or congenital heart disease
Patients with no history of surgery
items.explanation: The passage mentions that presumed hematogenous seeding is seen in patients with debilitating conditions such as cardiovascular or congenital heart disease.

items.question: What is the average glucose concentration in CSF during enterococcal meningitis?
items.explanation: The passage states that the average glucose concentration in CSF during enterococcal meningitis is 28 mg/dL.

items.question: Which combination has traditionally been used as the standard of care for endovascular infections caused by enterococci?
options: A cell wall-active agent and an aminoglycoside
Combination therapy with vancomycin and ampicillin
items.explanation: Combination therapy with a cell wall-active agent and an aminoglycoside was the standard of care for endovascular infections caused by enterococci.

items.question: What is one reason combination therapy with a $\beta$-lactam or glycopeptide and an aminoglycoside is effective against enterococcal infections?
options: It prevents the development of resistance.
The aminoglycoside penetrates better due to cell-wall alterations by the other agent.
It reduces the MIC of both agents.
It increases the toxicity to human cells.
items.explanation: The synergistic effect is partly explained by increased penetration of the aminoglycoside into the bacterial cell due to cell-wall alterations produced by the $\beta$-lactam or glycopeptide.

items.question: What has become increasingly difficult in treating severe enterococcal infections, particularly those caused by E. faecium?
options: Attaining synergistic bactericidal activity due to antibiotic resistance.
items.explanation: Attaining synergistic bactericidal activity in the treatment of severe enterococcal infections has become increasingly difficult because of the development of resistance to virtually all antibiotics available for this purpose.

items.question: Which aminoglycoside is recommended for synergistic therapy in severe enterococcal infections due to its unique resistance profile?
items.explanation: Streptomycin is recommended because the resistance mechanisms causing high-level resistance to streptomycin do not affect gentamicin.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.question: Why should tobramycin never be used for the treatment of E. faecium infections?
options: Resistance is common due to a chromosomal encoded enzyme.
It has high nephrotoxicity in these cases.
items.explanation: Tobramycin should never be used for E. faecium infections because of the presence of a species-specific, tobramycin-modifying enzyme.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.question: Which antibiotic is recommended as first-line therapy for E. faecalis endocarditis in patients at risk for aminoglycoside toxicity?
items.explanation: Ampicillin plus ceftriaxone is recommended as first-line therapy for E. faecalis endocarditis in patients at risk for aminoglycoside toxicity.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.question: Which antibiotic is not approved by the FDA for the treatment of VRE infections but may be used as an alternative?
items.explanation: Linezolid is the only agent approved by the FDA for the treatment of VRE infections, but it may be used as an alternative in certain severe cases.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following agents is not recommended for monotherapy in endovascular or severe enterococcal infections due to low attainable blood levels?
items.explanation: Tigecycline is not recommended as monotherapy for endovascular or severe enterococcal infections due to low attainable blood levels.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.question: Which antimicrobial agent has been shown to have improved outcomes with high-dose therapy compared to standard-dose therapy for VRE bacteremia?
items.explanation: High-dose daptomycin (≥10 mg/kg) has been associated with improved outcomes compared to standard-dose therapy for VRE bacteremia.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following agents is approved by the FDA only for uncomplicated urinary tract infections caused by vancomycin-susceptible E. faecalis?
items.explanation: Daptomycin is approved by the FDA only for uncomplicated urinary tract infections caused by vancomycin-susceptible E. faecalis.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.question: Which antimicrobial agent has been reported to have lower rates of all-cause mortality at 30 days and less microbiologic failure compared to linezolid in a large retrospective observational study?
items.explanation: A large retrospective observational study reported lower rates of all-cause mortality at 30 days and less microbiologic failure with daptomycin compared to linezolid.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.question: Which protein is primarily responsible for ampicillin resistance in Enterococcus faecium?
items.explanation: Ampicillin resistance in E. faecium is related to PBP5, which has low affinity for ampicillin.

items.question: What mechanism allows Enterococcus faecium to synthesize its cell wall despite the presence of ampicillin?
options: Low affinity of PBP5 for ampicillin
High affinity of PBP4 for vancomycin
items.explanation: PBP5 in E. faecium exhibits low affinity for ampicillin, allowing cell wall synthesis despite the antibiotic.

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of high-level ampicillin-resistant Enterococcus faecium strains?
options: Increased hydrolysis of ampicillin by PBP5
High affinity of PBP4 for penicillin
items.explanation: High-level ampicillin-resistant strains show increased production and dynamic binding pocket changes in PBP5, leading to increased hydrolysis of ampicillin.

items.statement: Enterococci are gram-negative organisms.
items.explanation: Enterococci are gram-positive organisms.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · Enterococcal Infections | MICROBIOLOGY AND TAXONOMY

items.statement: The majority of clinically relevant enterococcal species hydrolyze pyrrolidonyl-β-naphthylamide (PYR).
items.explanation: Most clinically important enterococci do hydrolyze PYR.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · Enterococcal Infections | MICROBIOLOGY AND TAXONOMY

items.statement: Disruption of the mucin layer and stomach pH can facilitate enterococcal access to the intestinal epithelium.
items.explanation: Medications that suppress stomach acid secretion or degrade the mucin layer can disrupt protective barriers, facilitating pathogen access.

items.statement: Hospital-associated strains of E. faecium are more adapted for survival in the gastrointestinal tract than commensal strains.
items.explanation: In the absence of antibiotics, hospital-associated lineages of E. faecium seem less adapted for survival compared to commensal strains.

items.statement: Enterococcal cytolysin is capable of lysing ovine RBCs.
items.explanation: Enterococcal cytolysin can lyse human and equine but not ovine RBCs.

items.statement: The aggregation substance of E. faecalis facilitates conjugative plasmid exchange.
items.explanation: Aggregation substance mediates the attachment of bacterial cells to each other, facilitating conjugative plasmid exchange.

items.statement: VRE colonization is not linked to prolonged hospitalization.
items.explanation: Prolonged hospitalization is an important factor associated with VRE colonization.

items.statement: VRE infection increases the risk of death compared to infections by glycopeptide-susceptible enterococcal strains.
items.explanation: Meta-analyses have found that VRE infection increases the risk of death over glycopeptide-susceptible enterococcal strains.

items.statement: The genus Enterococcus appeared approximately 400 million years ago with the advent of terrestrial animals.
items.explanation: Sequence analysis suggests the genus appeared ~400 million years ago with the advent of terrestrial animals.

items.statement: Clade B isolates are more likely to carry drug resistance determinants than clade A.
items.explanation: Strains belonging to clade A are more frequently identified as carrying drug resistance determinants, whereas clade B largely retains a susceptible phenotype.

items.statement: Removal of an indwelling catheter may suffice to eradicate enterococcal UTI without specific antimicrobial therapy.
items.explanation: In many cases, removing the indwelling catheter can eradicate the organism without specific antimicrobial therapy.

items.statement: Enterococcal bacteremia is most commonly caused by E. faecalis in patients with no apparent risk factors.
items.explanation: E. faecalis is a leading cause of endocarditis after TAVI, but enterococcal bacteremia often involves other sources and comorbidities.

items.statement: Enterococci are rarely involved in nosocomial surgical-site infections.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that enterococci rank third as agents of nosocomial surgical-site infections.

items.statement: Enterococcal meningitis is commonly associated with overt meningeal signs.
items.explanation: The passage notes that fever and changes in mental status are common, whereas overt meningeal signs are less so.

items.statement: Resistance to ampicillin and vancomycin is common in E. faecalis.
items.explanation: Resistance to ampicillin and vancomycin is rare in E. faecalis.

items.statement: Removal of indwelling intravascular catheters can increase the likelihood of enterococcal eradication.
items.explanation: Removal of the catheter increases the likelihood of enterococcal eradication by a subsequent short course of appropriate antimicrobial therapy.

items.statement: Aminoglycoside monotherapy should not be employed for enterococcal infections.
items.explanation: Aminoglycoside monotherapy is ineffective and should not be used; synergistic therapy with a $\beta$-lactam is recommended.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.statement: Vancomycin is the first-line treatment for E. faecalis infections.
items.explanation: Aminopenicillins are more active than vancomycin for treating E. faecalis infections.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.statement: Daptomycin is useful for treating pulmonary infections.
items.explanation: Daptomycin is not useful against pulmonary infections because the pulmonary surfactant inhibits its antibacterial activity.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.statement: Oritavancin has been approved for treating acute bacterial skin and soft tissue infections caused by VRE.
items.explanation: Oritavancin is approved for the treatment of acute bacterial skin and soft tissue infections caused by susceptible organisms, including vancomycin-susceptible E. faecalis, with low MICs against VRE.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.statement: Vancomycin resistance was first documented in Enterococcus faecium in the year 1986.
items.explanation: The first isolates of VRE were indeed documented in 1986.

items.statement: Ampicillin resistance is common in Enterococcus faecalis.
items.explanation: Ampicillin resistance is extremely rare in E. faecalis, with an emerging phenotype of penicillin-resistant but ampicillin-susceptible strains.

items.assertion: The majority of human infections caused by enterococci are due to E. faecalis and E. faecium.
items.reason: These species have been isolated most frequently from human infections.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · Enterococcal Infections | MICROBIOLOGY AND TAXONOMY

items.assertion: Enterococci can enhance the virulence of Clostridioides difficile.
items.reason: They alter the availability of fermentable amino acids and promote C. difficile fitness.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains how enterococci enhance C. difficile virulence.

items.assertion: Enterococcal colonization resistance is solely due to physical barriers like stomach pH.
items.reason: Colonization resistance involves metabolic and immunologic interactions beyond just physical factors.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because colonization resistance arises from complex interactions, not solely physical barriers.

items.assertion: Enterococcus faecalis mutants lacking gelatinase and serine protease are highly attenuated in experimental animal models.
items.reason: These proteins mediate virulence by degrading host tissues and modifying the immune system.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why mutants lacking these proteins are attenuated.

items.assertion: Teichoic acids on the enterococcal surface are associated with pathogenicity.
items.reason: Antibodies to teichoic acids are protective in some animal models.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and antibodies to teichoic acids being protective supports their association with pathogenicity.

items.assertion: The prevalence of hospital-associated VRE infections in Europe has increased from 8.1% in 2012 to 17.6% in 2022.
items.reason: This increase is due to the higher levels of human antibiotic use in Europe compared to other regions.
items.explanation: The increase in VRE prevalence is not attributed to higher levels of human antibiotic use in Europe; it's more prevalent in the United States.

items.assertion: E. faecalis is less commonly resistant to vancomycin than E. faecium.
items.reason: Vancomycin resistance in E. faecalis isolates is more prevalent in acute care hospitals compared to long-term care facilities.
items.explanation: E. faecalis has a higher prevalence of vancomycin resistance in long-term care facilities than in acute care hospitals.

items.assertion: Enterococci are important causes of both community- and health care-associated endocarditis.
items.reason: E. faecalis is the leading cause of endocarditis after transcatheter aortic valve implantation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why enterococci are important causes of endocarditis.

items.assertion: Enterococcal bacteremia is less severe when caused by E. faecium compared to other species.
items.reason: E. faecium has a higher prevalence of vancomycin and ampicillin resistance, leading to worse outcomes.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because E. faecium bacteremia leads to worse outcomes due to its resistance profile.

items.assertion: Enterococci have become prominent as a cause of intraabdominal infections in hospitalized patients.
items.reason: This is due to the emergence and spread of vancomycin resistance among enterococci.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Specific treatment for enterococci is beneficial in all cases of intraabdominal infections.
items.reason: Enterococci are part of the commensal microbiota and often do not require targeted therapy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true as enterococci are part of the commensal microbiota.

items.assertion: Use of amikacin is discouraged in the treatment of enterococcal infections.
items.reason: Amikacin is infrequently active against enterococci.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why amikacin use is discouraged.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.assertion: Daptomycin has FDA approval for treating VRE infections.
items.reason: Daptomycin shows in vitro activity against all enterococci.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because daptomycin is not approved by the FDA for VRE; however, it does have in vitro activity against all enterococci.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.assertion: Daptomycin breakpoints for E. faecium were changed by the CLSI in 2019.
items.reason: Mutations associated with daptomycin resistance are not uncommon and affect efficacy at lower dosing regimens.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the presence of mutations affecting daptomycin efficacy led to changes in breakpoints.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.reason: Telavancin, a lipoglycopeptide, has FDA approval for hospital-associated pneumonia and skin infections.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because telavancin is not active against VRE; however, the reason is true regarding its FDA approvals.
items.reference: Enterococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)
